coredata.dc:title: Glutathione-responsive PEGylated GQD-based nanomaterials for diagnosis and treatment of breast cancer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry

Lee, Yong-kyu
Oh, Seung Jun
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots-based nanoparticles (GQD-NPs) with reduction-responsive properties were synthesized via conjugation with two precursors, doxorubicin-disulfide-GQD that endowed the drug loading capability and polyethylene glycol-disulfide-Herceptin that enhanced the half-life and conferred active targeting ability towards HER2-overexpressed cancer. Disulfide linkages between the compartments are cleaved at a physiologically relevant concentration of glutathione in cancer cells, resulting a controlled release of drugs. In vitro analysis confirmed successful internalization of GQD-NPs in the cytoplasm and enhanced cellular uptake of DOX in HER2-positive breast cancer cells. The GQD-NPs show potential as a theranostic agent for HER2-overexpressed breast cancer cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
PEGylation
Stimuli-responsive degradation
Glutathione
Bio-imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Loading and GSH-triggered release of DOX from GQD-NPs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education ( NRF-2016R1A6A3A11934989 ), Development Program of the National Research Foundation funded by the Korea Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning ( NRF-2017M2A2A6A01070927 ) and Korea Health Technology R&D Project through the Korea Health Industry Development Institute (KHIDI), funded by the Ministry of Health & Welfare, Republic of Korea (grant number: HI18C2383 ).

item-info.ce:copyright: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and illustration of GQD-NPs and drug release via cellular uptake in human breast cancer cells.
Synthesis route of GQD-NPs: carboxylation of nGQD (a), conjugation of DOX (b), carboxylation of PEG (c), conjugation of HCT, and coupling reaction to form GQD-NPs (e).
Characterization of DOX-SS-GQD, PEG-SS-HCT, and GQD-NP by UV/Vis spectroscopy (a) and confirmation of synthesis of PEG-(SS-COOH)2 by 1H NMR (b).

(a) In vitro release profile of DOX from GQD-NPs in 10mM GSH and PBS as a control, (b) DLS diagrams of GQD-NPs solutions before and after treatment with GSH solution, (c) schematic illustration of GSH-triggered degradation of GQD-NPs, and (d) release profile of nGQD and DOX in the presence of 10mM GSH determine by UV/Vis spectroscopy.
CLSM images (scale bar=10μm) of SK-BR-3 and MDA-MB-231 cells incubated with GQD-NPs and free DOX for 8h.
Viability of SK-BR-3 and MDA-MB-231 cells incubated with GQD-NPs and free DOX for 48h determined by CCK-8 assay. Data are presented as the average±standard deviation (n=5).

head.ce:title: Glutathione-responsive PEGylated GQD-based nanomaterials for diagnosis and treatment of breast cancer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Green Bioengineering, Korea National University of Transportation, 50 Daehak-ro, Daesowon-myeon, Cheongju, Chungcheongbuk-do 27469, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Dental Materials, School of Dentistry, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots-based nanoparticles (GQD-NPs) with reduction-responsive properties were synthesized via conjugation with two precursors, doxorubicin-disulfide-GQD that endowed the drug loading capability and polyethylene glycol-disulfide-Herceptin that enhanced the half-life and conferred active targeting ability towards HER2-overexpressed cancer. Disulfide linkages between the compartments are cleaved at a physiologically relevant concentration of glutathione in cancer cells, resulting a controlled release of drugs. In vitro analysis confirmed successful internalization of GQD-NPs in the cytoplasm and enhanced cellular uptake of DOX in HER2-positive breast cancer cells. The GQD-NPs show potential as a theranostic agent for HER2-overexpressed breast cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer is one of the most fatal and common invasive cancers [1]. Chemotherapy has been used as the major treatment method as it causes remission of cancer and destroys nodular aggregates floating in the blood vessels, decreasing the chance of metastasis. However, conventional chemotherapeutic agents exert high toxicity to normal cells and cause severe side effects. Several platforms have been developed to overcome such limitations [2–4]. Although many of these platforms are effective, quantum dots (QDs) have attracted significant attention as novel drug delivery systems due to their unique intrinsic chemical and physical properties for treating and diagnosing breast cancer [5–9]. Initially, inorganic QDs were proposed as theranostic agents. They exhibit lower photobleaching, narrower photoluminescence spectra, and higher chemical stability [10]. However, as they are composed of inorganic materials such as cadmium, zinc, or selenium, their toxicity has been a critical barrier to the clinical translation [11]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), on the other hand, are suitable for cancer therapy as they are highly biocompatible while maintaining stable photoluminescence properties [12,13]. For instance, GQDs containing 10–40% oxygen are biocompatible and show favorable optical properties for the diagnosis of cancer at low cost [14].

The targeting ability is an important consideration in the design of drug delivery systems (DDS). A tumor-targeting ligand plays an important role in recognizing receptors on the surface of tumor cells. For example, Herceptin® (HCT or trastuzumab), a clinically used antibody, can act as a targeting moiety for patients with HER2-overexpressing cancer; these patients exhibit overexpressed HER2 receptors on the breast cancer cell surface. Conjugating HCT on the GQDs can significantly enhance the active targeting efficacy and reduce side effects because the complex can distinguish between healthy cells and cancer cells. Additionally, receptor-mediated endocytosis can significantly increase the rate of internalization by breast cancer cells. This may particularly be effective for treating resistant breast cancer.

An ideal GQD-based DDS should have excellent colloidal stability in the blood vessels. Conjugation of bulky HER can significantly decrease colloidal stability in the body. As a measure to improve aqueous solubility, polyethylene glycol (PEG) can be incorporated to provide flexibility and hydrophilicity [15,16]. In addition, the PEG moiety provides “stealth” effect to help evading the immune system [17,18]. Therefore, hydrophobic drugs in the core of the GQDs-PEG-HER complex can be protected from degradation and rapid removal by renal filtration [19].

To achieve on-demand release of encapsulated therapeutic agents, a stimuli-responsive degradation (SRD) platform can be introduced into the GQD complex. The versatile SRD platform undergoes chemical transition upon external stimuli. GQD-based colloids with dynamic covalent linkages can be destabilized in a controlled manner [20,21]. A few examples of SRD platforms include endogenous stimuli-responsive systems (pH [22], glutathione (GSH) [23], reactive oxygen species [24], enzyme [25]) and exogenous stimuli-responsive systems (light [26], temperature [27], magnetic field [28]). GSH-responsive systems are especially promising, as a higher concentration of GSH is found in intracellular compartments (2–10mM) of cancer cells as opposed to extracellular compartments (<20μM) [29,30]. To date, few GSH-responsive GQDs have been reported [31–33]. The reported platforms have a stimuli-responsive component at a single location and demonstrated a relatively slow release rate of encapsulated drugs. Disulfide linkages can be placed at multiple locations to achieve more rapid release of therapeutics [34], but a multi-location GSH-responsive GQD system has never been reported.

Herein, novel GSH-responsive degradable GQD-based nanoparticles (GQD-NPs) were successfully synthesized for breast cancer therapy and diagnosis (Scheme 1 ). The GQD-NPs were designed to contain doxorubicin (DOX), GQDs, and PEGylated HCT covalently conjugated through disulfide linkages. Direct conjugation of DOX provides optimal loading of DOX. PEG moiety endows stealth effects to the GQD-NPs and HCT enables selective targeting ability for HER2-overexpressing breast cancer. The results showed that, upon exposure to physiologically relevant concentrations of GSH in cancer cells, the disulfide linkages between the blocks were cleaved. Encapsulated DOX was then rapidly released from GQD-NPs in a controlled manner. The confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) and cell viability measurements revealed anticancer activity and enhanced cellular uptake of GQD-NPs in SK-BR-3 cells (HER2-positive breast cancer cells) compared to MDB-MB-231 cells (HER2-negative breast cancer cells). These results suggest that GQD-NPs have excellent potential as a theranostic agent for the diagnosis and treatment of HER2-overexpressing breast cancer.

N-doped GQDs (nGQDs) were synthesized by pyrolysis of L-glutamic acid and the detailed procedure was described in our previous report [12]. 3,3′-Dithiodipropionic acid (ss-COOH), 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC), doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX), polyethylene glycol (PEG, MW=3000g/mol), trimethylamine (Et3N), tetrahydrofuran (THF), 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES), and sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Herceptin (HCT) was purchased from Roche (Basel, Switzerland). Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640, fetal bovine serum (FBS), Dulbecco’s phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), Trypsin-EDTA, penicillin, and streptomycin were purchased from GIBCO BRL (Grand Island, NY, USA). Lysotracker (Product # L7526) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA). Unless otherwise indicated, all chemicals were used without further purification. UV/Vis spectra were collected on a Shimadzu UV-1650PC (Kyoto, Japan). The number-average diameter of particles was measured using a Malvern Instruments Zetasizer Nano S 90 (ZEN1690, Malvern, UK).

DOX-SS-GQDs were synthesized in situ. A mixture of nGQD (18.5mg) and EDC (110.7mg, 0.71mmol) in PBS (5mL) was added to a solution of SS-COOH (100mg, 0.48mmol) and DMAP (87.2mg, 0.72mmol) in 5mL PBS. The resulting mixture was stirred at 25°C for 12h and a solution of DOX (3.6mg), Et3N (4.82μL, 3 molar equivalents to DOX), EDC (33.3mg, 0.21mmol), and DMAP (33.3mg, 0.27mmol) in 5mL PBS was added. The mixture was stirred at RT for 12h and dialyzed using dialysis tubing (MWCO=1000g/mol) against distilled water (DW) for 2days to remove residues; the samples were lyophilized and stored until use. The loading capacity (LC) of DOX was calculated by the following formulas. Briefly, an aliquot of the GQD-NPs was mixed with 3mL DMF and its UV/Vis spectra was measured at λmax =496nm.
(1)
L C =
w e i g h t
o f
D O X
i n
G Q D − N P s
t o t a l
w e i g h t
o f
G Q D − N P s

PEG-SS-HCT was synthesized in two steps. First, PEG-(SS-COOH)2 was synthesized by a click reaction. SS-COOH (630.8mg, 3.0mmol) was dissolved in 10mL THF in the presence of DMAP (36.7mg, 0.3mmol). A solution of PEG 3000 (3.0g, 1.0mmol) and EDC (465.7mg, 3.0mmol) in 10mL THF was added and stirred at RT for 24h. The mixture was then filtered and concentrated by rotary evaporation and then dialyzed using dialysis tubing (MWCO=3500g/mol) against DW for 3days followed by lyophilization. The dried PEG-(SS-COOH)2 (820mg, 0.24mmol) was dissolved in 10mL PBS in the presence of EDC (51mg, 0.33mmol). HCT (31mg) and DMAP (10mg, 0.08mmol) were dissolved in 10mL PBS and added dropwise into the solution while stirring at room temperature. The reaction was allowed to proceed for 14h and then dialyzed using dialysis tubing (MWCO=3500g/mol) against DW for 3 days. The purified PEG-SS-HCT was then lyophilized and stored at 4°C until use.

A mixture of 150mg of PEG-SS-HCT and DMAP (65mg, 0.54mmol) dissolved in 20mL PBS was added to a solution of DOX-SS-GQD and EDC (832mg, 5.36mmol) in 10mL PBS. The resulting mixture was stirred at RT for 24h and dialyzed using dialysis tubing (MWCO=3500g/mol) against DW for 3 days. The product was then lyophilized and stored at 4°C until analysis.

SK-BR-3 and MDA-MB-231 cells, which are human breast cancer cell lines, were purchased from Korean Cell Line Bank (Seoul, South Korea) and cultured in DMEM with L-glutamine (300mg/L) containing 10% FBS, and 1% antibiotics (50U/mL penicillin and 50U/mL streptomycin) at 37°C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2.

SK-BR-3 and MDA-MB-231 cells plated at a density of 5×103 cells/dish in a confocal dish were incubated at 37°C for 24h. Cells were treated with 50μg/mL GQD-NPs and FREE-DOX (at the same molar concentration) in PBS and 2μM LysoTracker Green DND-26 at 37°C for 8h. The dishes were washed with PBS three times to remove residues and the cells were fixed with 4% formaldehyde solution at RT for 20min. The images were obtained by CLSM (Zeiss LSM 780, Carl Zeiss, Jena, Germany).

SK-BR-3 and MDA-MB-231 cells were plated at 5×103 cells/well into a 96-well plate and cultured in DMEM (100μL) containing 10% FBS and 1% antibiotics for 24h. Cells were then treated with GQD-NPs and free DOX for 48h, and then 100μL of CCK solution was added to each well and the plate was incubated for 2h. Absorbance was measured at 450nm using a Benchmark Plus microplate spectrophotometer (BR170-6930, Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA).
Scheme 2 illustrates the synthetic approach used to prepare GQD-based NPs (GQD-NPs) containing DOX and HCT.

First, DOX and GQD were conjugated by a disulfide linkage in a facile EDC/DMAP coupling reaction in situ, producing DOX-SS-GQD. Amine groups on the surface of the nGQD were covalently attached to SS-COOH to introduce terminal carboxyl groups. These carboxyl groups enabled conjugation to the secondary amine on DOX via a facile coupling reaction. Direct conjugation of DOX enabled increased loading of GQD-NPs, leading to improved antitumor activity at a low concentration of GQD-NPs. HCT was linked to PEG and disulfide cleavable linkages at junctions. PEG improved not only the aqueous solubility of the GQD-NPs, but also the mobility of the bulky HCT. Additionally, disulfide bonds can be degraded to the corresponding thiols in response to the reductive environment in cancer cells, resulting controlled drug release.

Successful synthesis of GQD-NPs was confirmed by UV/Vis and 1H NMR (Fig. 1 ). As shown in Fig. 1a, UV/Vis spectra of pristine DOX showed a broad peak at 497nm. DOX-SS-GQD showed two broad peaks at 320nm, corresponding to sp2 clusters in nGQD [12] and at 497nm, indicating conjugation of DOX. In addition, synthesis of DOX-SS-GQD conjugates was characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy [35]. DOX showed two emission peaks with different intensities at 559 and 592nm (Fig. S1). After conjugation with nGQD, these peaks were shifted (556 and 584nm) and the intensity of each peak showed an opposite trend from the other. Such differences in the emission spectra revealed successful DOX conjugation on the nGQD surface. The synthesis of PEG-(SS-COOH)2 was confirmed by 1H NMR (Fig. 1b). Because the UV-active moiety is not present in the chemical structure of PEG-(SS-COOH)2, HCT-conjugation for the formation of PEG-SS-HCT could only be confirmed by UV/Vis spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1a, PEG-SS-HCT showed two broad peaks at 280 and 500nm, corresponding to HCT and DOX, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading and GSH-triggered release of DOX from GQD-NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The loading capacity (LC) was determined to be 23.1% using the Beer-Lambert equation with the absorbance at λmax =496nm and extinction coefficient (ε=12,800M−1 cm−1) in DW/DMF=1/3 (v/v), as reported in our previous publication [12].

The release profile of DOX from GQD-NPs was investigated by mimicking the intracellular conditions. An aliquot of GQD-NPs was added to a dialysis tubing and incubated in a solution consisting of 10mM of GSH or in a PBS solution as a control at 37°C. Fig. 2 a shows that no significant release of DOX was observed in the absence of GSH. On the other hand, >60% of DOX release was observed when the GSH concentration was increased to 10mM. The size of resulting micellar aggregates were characterized using DLS. As shown in Fig. 2b, a monomodal distribution of GQD-NPs was observed with an average diameter=138.0±2.7nm. In the presence of GSH, large aggregates were observed suggesting that the disulfide linkages were cleaved and destabilized the GQD-NPs. Furthermore, degradation of GQD-NPs involves not only the release of DOX but also nGQD. As shown in Fig. 2c, cleavage of disulfide bonds of GQD-NPs in the presence of 10mM GSH released both DOX and nGQD, which are UV-active. UV spectra of the released nGQD at 320nm were collected and compared with the release profile of DOX (Fig. 2d). GQD-NPs rapidly responded to GSH, leading to the release of DOX shown by an increase in absorbance at 498nm. This enhanced release is attributed to the destabilization of GQD-NPs by the cleavages of disulfide linkages between the junctions of compartments. These results suggest that GQD-NPs would not cause undesired accumulation at non-targeted sites as the degraded nGQD and Dox can be readily removed by renal filtration system.

The usefulness of GQD-NPs as Herceptin-overexpressed breast cancer-targeting theranostic agents was evaluated by using two different breast cancer cells: HER2-positive SK-BR-3 and HER2-negative MDA-MB-231 cell lines. First, intracellular trafficking of GQD-NPs and DOX was examined by CLSM. Fig. 3 shows the CLSM images of SK-BR-3 incubated with GQD-NPs and free DOX for 8h. SK-BR-3 cells incubated with GQD-NPs displayed strong blue-color fluorescence in the cytoplasm as compared to MDA-MB-231 cells, indicating good cellular uptake of GQD-NPs. Additionally, the intensity of DOX from GQD-NPs was nearly the same as that of free DOX, indicating that the GQD-NPs delivered the anticancer drugs into the nuclei of cancer cells and effectively inhibited cancer cell growth. These results support that GQD-NPs have great potential not only as bio-imaging probes, but also as drug carriers for the diagnosis and the treatment of breast cancer.

Based the promising results above, the in vitro cytotoxicity of GQD-NPs was evaluated by comparison with free DOX in a CCK-8 assay (Fig. 4 ). The viability of HER2-positive SK-BR-3 cells was significantly decreased to <50% at 50μg/mL GQD-NPs, whereas the viability of MDA-MB-231 cells was reduced to >85%. The difference in viability between the two cell lines indicates that GQD-NPs were effectively internalized by HER2-mediated endocytosis targeting HER2-overexpressing cells. Moreover, DOX was released in a controlled manner upon the cleavage of disulfide linkages in response to a high concentration of GSH. In contrast, free DOX could not be used to distinguish the two cell lines. These promising results from in vitro cytotoxicity analysis confirmed that GQD-NPs can serve as a smart nanomaterial for selectively delivering and releasing DOX into the nuclei of breast cancer cells, leading to cancer cell apoptosis. This combination strategy of active targeting and controlled drug delivery can reduce side effects by avoiding undesired accumulation at non-target sites.

GSH-responsive degradable GQD-NPs were successfully synthesized as a promising breast cancer theranostic agent. The GQD-NPs were designed to contain covalently conjugated DOX and PEGylated HER2 antibody, with each component linked via GSH-responsive disulfide linkages. Direct conjugation of DOX provided the optimal loading level of DOX at a low concentration of GQD-NPs. The addition of PEGylated HCT conferred stealth effects by the PEG and selective targeting ability towards HER2-overexpressing breast cancer by the HCT antibody. The synthesis was confirmed by UV/Vis spectroscopy and 1H NMR spectroscopy analyses. Such triple location GSH-responsive GQD-based drug delivery system is expected to enhance rate of drug release and to promote rapid removal by body. This type of platform has never been introduced with GQDs. Prior to biological assessment, controlled release of multi-location reduction-responsive GQD-NPs was examined by UV spectroscopy. GQD-NPs rapidly degraded upon the cleavage of disulfide linkages between the junctions of compartments, increasing the release of DOX into the aqueous solution. To further assess the GQD-NPs as theranostic agents, antitumor activity and intracellular trafficking were explored in vitro using SK-BR-3 (HER2-positive) and MDA-MB-231 (HER2-negative) cells. CLSM results indicated enhanced cellular uptake of GQD-NPs by SK-BR-3 cells compared to that by MDA-MB-231 cells. Furthermore, the antitumor activity was greatly enhanced in the HER2-overexpressing cell line, whereas free DOX inhibited both cell lines. These results suggest that GQD-NPs have excellent selectivity towards breast cancer with upregulated HER2 expression.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2016R1A6A3A11934989), Development Program of the National Research Foundation funded by the Korea Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning (NRF-2017M2A2A6A01070927) and Korea Health Technology R&D Project through the Korea Health Industry Development Institute (KHIDI), funded by the Ministry of Health & Welfare, Republic of Korea (grant number: HI18C2383).

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent Developments in Nanoparticulate Drug Delivery Systems
